sourceMovie.title: Alice in Wonderland
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Alice in Wonderland (2010) as a family theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The family and fantasy blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Charlie and the Chocolate Factory
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Charlie and the Chocolate Factory (2005) captures the family appeal of Alice in Wonderland without simply imitating it. The fantasy streak running through both films is what puts Charlie and the Chocolate Factory at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Maleficent
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The family appeal of Alice in Wonderland — that uplifting edge, the fantasy — translates cleanly into Maleficent (2014). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: The Jungle Book
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind The Jungle Book (2016) clearly understood what makes family work — the same quality that keeps Alice in Wonderland rewatchable. Expect the same uplifting rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: Alice Through the Looking Glass
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Alice Through the Looking Glass (2016) is the kind of film Alice in Wonderland fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The family overlap is real, but it's the uplifting emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Maleficent: Mistress of Evil
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Maleficent: Mistress of Evil shares Alice in Wonderland's gift for family — the 2019 film delivers fantasy without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Alice in Wonderland
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Alice in Wonderland compelling — the family, the fantasy — shows up in Alice in Wonderland (1951) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that uplifting storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: The Little Mermaid
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The family energy of Alice in Wonderland is alive in The Little Mermaid (2023), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a uplifting payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Descendants 2
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Descendants 2 (2017) operates in the same family as Alice in Wonderland, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The uplifting payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Pinocchio
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Pinocchio (2022) belongs to the same conversation as Alice in Wonderland — both anchor their family in fantasy emotion that doesn't let go. The uplifting undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Flora & Ulysses
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Alice in Wonderland's family stayed with you, Flora & Ulysses (2021) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The fantasy through-line is the real common thread between them.

faq.question: What should I watch after Alice in Wonderland?
faq.answer: Try Charlie and the Chocolate Factory, Maleficent, The Jungle Book—each shares DNA with Alice in Wonderland while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Alice in Wonderland?
faq.answer: Alice in Wonderland (2010) is best described as Family, Fantasy, Adventure. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Alice in Wonderland part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Alice in Wonderland Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
